column18:
Town Hall Meeting Survey
Informed Consent

Hello Chico Student:

The following online survey is part of an important university study on how to improve students' experiences at the Town Hall Meeting. It will take approximately 5-10 minutes to complete.

Taking part in this research is completely voluntary. If you choose to participate in this research, it is very important that you answer the questions as thoughtfully and frankly as possible. All your answers will be kept strictly confidential. You can stop participating at any time without penalty to your grade.
We also emphasize that you will NOT be penalized if you choose to omit answers to some questions on the survey. To record your answers, you must proceed all the way through the end of the survey, even if you choose to omit answers to some questions. In addition, proceeding to the very end of the survey will remove any survey response information from the screen on your computer/device, protecting your privacy. 

Your student ID is being collected so we can match your responses to other important university programs (U-Course, Town Hall Meeting, Great Debate) and demographic information. IDs may be used for statistical analysis alongside other data such as GPA, retention, and graduation rates. Again, your answers are strictly confidential, and we are bound by the rules of this University to maintain this confidentiality. Your information collected as part of this research will be stored on a password-protected computer in a locked office within the First-Year Experience Program office and on a protected server that is located behind a secure firewall at the University Office for Institutional Research. You must be 18 years of age or older to take this survey.

Thank you for filling out this survey as completely and honestly as you can. We are asking you to do this because we value your experience and ideas about how to improve students' experiences here at Chico State.

If you'd like more information about this project, contact the First-Year Experience office at fye@csuchico.edu or (530) 898-3705.

Demographic Data:
This data will help Undergraduate Studies, First-Year Experience, and the POLS department to better understand student populations as they are now, where they've been, and where they're headed.  This information is a powerful tool for tracking change over time, uncovering the needs and strengths of our student population, and will help guide course planning, policy development, funding requests, and University and department decision-making.

column56:
There are three parts to the Town Hall meeting. 

The first part was the Civic Engagement and it was not well run/managed. There were 400 people told to roam around in a small area and find a booth that fit our policy topic. There was no booth that I saw that really fit my topic so I asked to help desk. They told me what booth to go to, but because it was not well run/managed there was just a mob of people around this one booth run by one girl and when I asked about my policy topic she said it was not in her realm of expertise and she could not speak to it. So on my sheet, I just had to write down N/A.

The second part was the Break Out Rooms where we met with people of like topic. I finished at the Civic Engagement early apparently because I was the first one in the room by about 15 minutes. Then when everyone got there and began to talk about their more narrow topic we discovered that all of our topics for the most part were different. So the first break out was the whole class and for the most part, it was one student and the "expert" talking/arguing back and forth. The second breakout did not go much better. The mediator and the experts were supposed to bounce around and make sure the groups stayed on topic but no one really talked to my group and no one in my group would say anything of substance. They would make generalized statements that no one can disagree with like "human trafficking and drugs are bad so we need more border security". That's great but then no one would offer up how to increase border security in a good way and anytime I tried the topic would change to another broad sweeping statement.

The final part was trying to get students aware of how they could enact change in the community. This was done fairly well, I don't really know how I would have done it differently so that was probably the best part of the night for me. It was somewhat useful information and since the groups were split between Colusa and the Library then I was actually able to have a conversation and learn something.

I met a lot of new people/friends which I loved the most about it. Some of the rooms and spaces were super small to hold a lot of the people there, but I do understand just because of the limited space available. If I had a choice to go, I probably would not as I didn’t feel confident in my work prior to it, but I did like going so it could help my grade in the long run. One reason why I did not feel confident is because I had filled out some sections incorrectly and didn’t have a lot of time to get it worked on. column56: If I am going to be brutally honest, this was one of the worst ran events I have attended. The beginning of the event forced people to wander around the cramped spaces of the library like headless chickens. Only having small signs to say what organization was on the table proved to be completely ineffective since everyone was walking around. Nobody knew where they were going unless they somehow stumbled upon a table that may or may not have been related to their policy area. The small groups were alright, but they ran out of cookies for our section which is very minor, but disappointing nonetheless. Once we got back to the library, it was just as disorganized and chaotic as it was during the start. The way the tables were set up made it so it was hard to actually sit down and listen to what people had to say, people who were volunteering their time at that. It beats me why this wasn't held at Colusa hall or the BMU where it could have been much more organized with more room to work with. Overall, this proved to be a very disappointing result with all of the work that led up to it.
